title: Conjecture 1.3 (Li et al.)
statement_text: Every $Z_3$-flow-critical graph $G$ satisfies $|E(G)| \leq 3|V(G)|-5$. Moreover, if $|V(G)| \geq 7$, then $|E(G)| \leq 3|V(G)| - 8$.
context_text: Li et al. observed that the planar upper bound $|E(G)| \leq \frac{5|V(G)|-8}{2}$ cannot extend to non-planar graphs: for $n \geq 7$ the graph $K^+_{3,n-3}$ is $Z_3$-flow-critical with $3n-8$ edges. They conjectured this construction gives the worst case in general. The paper notes the version without the restriction $|V(G)| \geq 7$ follows by inspection of small graphs.
paper_contribution: Proves Conjecture 1.3 for every $Z_3$-flow-critical graph $G$ whose Euler genus satisfies $g(G) \leq (|V(G)|-8)/5$ via Theorem 1.5; shows the conjecture can be verified on any fixed surface by examining finitely many graphs; proves (Theorem 1.9) that Conjecture 1.8 (Jaeger et al.) implies the first part $|E(G)| \leq 3|V(G)|-5$.

title: Problem 1.6
statement_text: What is the smallest constant $\alpha$ such that every $Z_3$-flow-critical graph $G$ satisfies $|E(G)| \leq \frac{5}{2}|V(G)| + \alpha g(G) + O(1)$?
context_text: Theorem 1.5 establishes $\alpha \leq 5/2$, while the example of $K^+_{3,n-3}$ (which has Euler genus $\lceil\frac{n-5}{2}\rceil$) shows $\alpha \geq 1$, giving $1 \leq \alpha \leq 5/2$. The authors remark that the dependence on genus in Theorem 1.5 is likely not optimal.

title: Conjecture 1.8 (Jaeger et al.)
context_text: This is the group-connectivity analogue of Tutte's 3-flow conjecture; the 3-flow conjecture can be reduced to 5-edge-connected graphs and is implied by this stronger statement. Lovász et al. proved $Z_3$-connectedness for 6-edge-connected graphs.
paper_contribution: Proves Conjecture 1.8 for planar and projective-planar graphs as a corollary of Theorem 1.7 (since every flow-critical $Z_3$-bordered graph of Euler genus at most one has average degree less than five); also establishes it for graphs that can be made planar by removing at most three edges, even when the flow on those edges is prescribed arbitrarily.
